label: Chapter 6 - Deploying and Scaling Applications with Kubernetes

flashcards.question: What is the primary purpose of a Kubernetes Deployment?
flashcards.answer: A Deployment manages Pods and ReplicaSets to ensure declarative updates and lifecycle control. The chapter states: "Deployment is for controlling Pods and managing the application life cycle of those Pods."

flashcards.question: Why are Pods alone not resilient in Kubernetes?
flashcards.answer: Pods do not provide self-healing, rolling updates, or autoscaling. The text says: "Pods... don't provide any advanced Kubernetes features, such as self-healing, rolling updates, or autoscaling."

flashcards.question: What does a ReplicaSet ensure?
flashcards.answer: It ensures a specified number of replica Pods are running. The chapter states: "A ReplicaSet is used to maintain the given number of replica Pods running at any given time."

flashcards.question: Why is Deployment preferred over directly using ReplicaSets?
flashcards.answer: Deployments provide update orchestration and higher-level management. The text says: "ReplicaSet does not allow you to implement custom update orchestration... use Deployments instead."

flashcards.question: What is the role of the selector field in a Deployment?
flashcards.answer: It defines how the Deployment finds the Pods it manages. The chapter states: "The selector... defines how this Deployment finds the pods that it manages."

flashcards.question: What happens when a Pod managed by a Deployment is deleted?
flashcards.answer: Kubernetes automatically creates a replacement Pod. The text says: "Kubernetes detected that... only two replicas were running... and spawned a new, third replica."

flashcards.question: What command is used to scale a Deployment?
flashcards.answer: kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas <n>. The chapter shows: "$ minikube kubectl -- scale deployment nginx-deployment --replicas 5".

flashcards.question: What is a rolling update in Kubernetes?
flashcards.answer: A zero-downtime method of updating application Pods. The text says: "Rolling updates... allow us to make updates with zero downtime for customers."

flashcards.question: How do you update a Deployment’s container image?
flashcards.answer: kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container>=<image>. The chapter shows: "kubectl -- set image deployment/nginx-deployment nginx=nginx:1.20".

flashcards.question: What does kubectl rollout undo do?
flashcards.answer: It rolls back a Deployment to a previous revision. The chapter states: "we can simply call kubectl rollout undo..."

flashcards.question: What does imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent mean?
flashcards.answer: The image is pulled only if not already cached. The text says: "IfNotPresent... image will be downloaded only if... not already present locally."

flashcards.question: What does imagePullPolicy: Always do?
flashcards.answer: Always checks the registry for the latest digest. The chapter states: "Always... every time a pod... is started, the image registry will be asked for an image digest."

flashcards.question: What does maxUnavailable control in a rolling update?
flashcards.answer: The maximum number of unavailable Pods during an update. The text says: "maxUnavailable... defines the maximum number of unavailable pods during a rolling update."

flashcards.question: What does maxSurge control in a rolling update?
flashcards.answer: How many extra Pods can be created beyond desired replicas. The chapter states: "maxSurge... defines the maximum number of pods that can be created over the desired number of replicas."

flashcards.question: What is the Recreate strategy in Deployments?
flashcards.answer: All old Pods are killed before new ones are created. The text says: "Recreate... all existing Pods are killed at once and new ones are only created after all old Pods have been terminated."

flashcards.question: What is a DaemonSet?
flashcards.answer: A controller ensuring one Pod per node. The chapter states: "DaemonSet... ensures that all or certain nodes... each run a single replica of the target pod."

flashcards.question: What are common DaemonSet use cases?
flashcards.answer: Log collection, monitoring, and storage daemons. The text lists: "log collection service... node-monitoring daemon... cluster storage daemon."

flashcards.question: Can you set replicas in a DaemonSet?
flashcards.answer: No. DaemonSets always run exactly one Pod per node. The chapter states: "DaemonSet will always run only one pod per node."

flashcards.question: Why do stateful applications need persistent storage?
flashcards.answer: Container filesystems are ephemeral. The text says: "Data written on a container filesystem is not persisted by default."

flashcards.question: What is a PersistentVolume (PV)?
flashcards.answer: A piece of storage in the cluster. The chapter states: "Persistent Volume (PV): This is a piece of storage in the cluster..."

flashcards.question: What is a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)?
flashcards.answer: A user request for storage. The text says: "Persistent VolumeClaim (PVC): This is a request for storage by the user..."

flashcards.question: What does ReadWriteOnce mean?
flashcards.answer: Volume can be mounted read-write by a single node. The chapter states: "ReadWriteOnce... mounted as a read-write by a single node."

flashcards.question: What does ReadWriteMany mean?
flashcards.answer: Volume can be mounted read-write by many nodes. The text says: "ReadWriteMany... mounted as read-write by many nodes."

flashcards.question: What is a StorageClass?
flashcards.answer: Defines storage types and provisioning behavior. The chapter states: "Storage classes provide a way to classify different storage options..."

flashcards.question: What happens if no PV matches a PVC?
flashcards.answer: Kubernetes dynamically provisions a PV. The text says: "Kubernetes can dynamically provision a PV based on the storage class..."

flashcards.question: What is a StatefulSet?
flashcards.answer: A controller for ordered, unique, stateful Pods. The chapter states: "StatefulSet... guarantees the ordering and uniqueness of these Pods."

flashcards.question: What naming behavior do StatefulSets provide?
flashcards.answer: Stable Pod names without random suffixes. The text says: "Pods created by StatefulSets have stable naming (without randomly generated UUIDs)."

flashcards.question: What does volumeClaimTemplates do in a StatefulSet?
flashcards.answer: Automatically provisions a PV per Pod replica. The chapter states: "StatefulSets can provision a PV per pod replica."

flashcards.question: What is a ConfigMap?
flashcards.answer: Stores non-confidential configuration data. The text says: "A ConfigMap is a resource to store non-confidential data..."

flashcards.question: What is a Secret?
flashcards.answer: Stores sensitive data like passwords and tokens. The chapter states: "A Secret is a resource to store sensitive data such as passwords, tokens..."

flashcards.question: How can ConfigMaps and Secrets be consumed?
flashcards.answer: As environment variables or mounted files. The text says: "consumed inside Pods as environment variables... or configuration files."

flashcards.question: Why shouldn’t configuration be baked into container images?
flashcards.answer: It requires rebuilding images for small changes. The chapter states: "If you do so... you'll have to rebuild container images."

flashcards.question: What is Kubernetes service discovery based on?
flashcards.answer: DNS. The text says: "service discovery mechanism based on Domain Name System (DNS)."

flashcards.question: What is a Service in Kubernetes?
flashcards.answer: An abstraction that exposes Pods and enables stable networking. The chapter states: "Service is an abstraction layer that enables loose coupling..."

flashcards.question: Why can’t clients rely on Pod IPs?
flashcards.answer: Pods get new IPs when recreated. The text says: "Pods... are recreated with a new name and a new IP address."

flashcards.question: What are labels in Kubernetes?
flashcards.answer: Key/value metadata attached to objects. The chapter states: "Labels are simple key/value metadata pairs..."

flashcards.question: What are selectors used for?
flashcards.answer: Selecting groups of objects by labels. The text says: "Selectors allow the identification of a set of Kubernetes objects."

flashcards.question: What is a ClusterIP Service?
flashcards.answer: Internal-only service accessible within the cluster. The chapter states: "ClusterIP... exposes an application on an internal cluster IP."

flashcards.question: What is a NodePort Service?
flashcards.answer: Exposes the app on a static port on each node. The text says: "NodePort... exposes the application on the same static port of each node."

flashcards.question: What is a LoadBalancer Service?
flashcards.answer: Exposes the app externally using a cloud load balancer. The chapter states: "LoadBalancer... exposes the application outside of cluster using a cloud provider's load balancer."

flashcards.question: What is an ExternalName Service?
flashcards.answer: Maps a service to an external DNS name. The text says: "ExternalName... maps the service to an external DNS name... returning a CNAME record."

flashcards.question: What is a probe in Kubernetes?
flashcards.answer: A diagnostic check performed by kubelet. The chapter states: "A probe is a diagnostic that is performed by a Kubernetes kubelet on a container."

flashcards.question: What is a liveness probe?
flashcards.answer: Checks if a container is alive; restarts it if not. The text says: "Liveness: Ensures that a process... is alive and, if not, restarts the container."

flashcards.question: What is a readiness probe?
flashcards.answer: Checks if a container is ready to accept traffic. The chapter states: "Readiness: Ensures that the application is ready to accept traffic."

flashcards.question: What is a startup probe?
flashcards.answer: Used for slow-starting apps; disables other probes until success. The text says: "Startup... disables liveness and readiness probes until it succeeds."

flashcards.question: What happens when a liveness probe fails?
flashcards.answer: The container is restarted. The chapter states: "Liveness probe failed... container... will be restarted."

flashcards.question: What happens when a readiness probe fails?
flashcards.answer: The Pod is removed from Service endpoints. The text says: "its pod IP is automatically taken out from the list of endpoints of the Service."

flashcards.question: What types of checks can probes perform?
flashcards.answer: Command, HTTP request, or TCP probe. The chapter states: "Each probe can execute a custom command, perform an HTTP request, or a TCP probe."

flashcards.question: What happens when a readiness probe fails in Kubernetes?
flashcards.answer: The Pod is temporarily removed from Service endpoints so it stops receiving traffic. The chapter states: "its pod IP is automatically taken out from the list of endpoints of the Service."

flashcards.question: What is the reconciliation loop?
flashcards.answer: Kubernetes continuously ensuring desired state matches current state. The text says: "Kubernetes reconciliation loop kicked in and spawned a new... replica."
